title: Effect of Tariffs on U.S. Small Businesses - Liberty Street Economics
gist: Using the 2025 Small Business Credit Survey, NY Fed researchers find small businesses are exposed to tariffs mainly through imported inputs rather than exports, that about 80% pass on at least some tariff-driven costs to customers while about 60% absorb some, and that firms hit hardest by tariffs are notably more pessimistic about 2026 employment and revenue.

themes.theme: Small business exposure runs through imports, not exports

insights.claim: Only about 30% of US goods firms and 20% of retail firms reported selling to international customers in 2024, but about 70% of goods firms and 80% of retail firms reported using at least some inputs sourced from outside the US, showing small-business tariff exposure is mainly on the import side.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Tariff policy aimed at protecting domestic producers from import competition still hits small firms hard as input-cost inflation, even though most don't export.
insights.locator: Exposure of U.S. Small Businesses to Imported Input Prices

insights.claim: In the New York/New Jersey/Connecticut Second District, 90% of manufacturers and 75% of services firms reported importing some goods, an even higher import-reliance rate than the national small-business average.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Regional Northeast small businesses are structurally more tariff-exposed than the national small-business population, a relevant regional-risk signal for local lenders and policymakers.
insights.locator: Exposure of U.S. Small Businesses to Imported Input Prices

insights.claim: Input tariffs have been shown in prior research to have larger effects on productivity than output tariffs, reinforcing why small firms' heavy reliance on imported inputs (more than their reliance on export sales) matters economically.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Suggests the productivity drag from current tariff policy could be larger than a simple trade-balance analysis would suggest, because it works through the input side.
insights.locator: Exposure of U.S. Small Businesses to Imported Input Prices

insights.claim: The largest 1% of US firms account for 90% of international trade by value, but the true importance of small businesses to trade is understated because it excludes indirect exports such as small firms selling via online intermediaries.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Standard trade statistics may undercount small-business exposure to tariff and trade policy shifts by missing indirect, platform-mediated export channels.
insights.locator: Exposure of U.S. Small Businesses to Imported Input Prices

themes.theme: Tariffs are a distinct, identifiable financial challenge

insights.claim: Nationally, 55% of goods firms, 67% of retail firms, and 34% of services firms cited tariff-related costs as a financial challenge in 2025; in the Second District the shares were higher at 62%, 72%, and 44% respectively.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Gives investors a sector-by-sector ranking of tariff pain: retail is hit hardest, services least, with the Northeast region consistently worse than the national average.
insights.locator: Incidence of Tariff-Related Costs on Small Businesses

insights.claim: Regression evidence shows that firms reporting tariff-related challenges (as opposed to firms reporting general inflation challenges) are the ones statistically associated with actually paying higher imported input prices in 2025, indicating firms can correctly distinguish tariff effects from broad inflation.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Validates that survey-reported 'tariff challenges' are a real, distinct cost driver rather than firms simply mislabeling general inflation — strengthens the credibility of using this survey to isolate tariff effects.
insights.locator: Incidence of Tariff-Related Costs on Small Businesses

insights.claim: About 80% of firms nationally reported that their imported input prices increased in 2025 relative to 2024.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: A high-frequency, ground-level confirmation that tariff pass-through into small-business input costs was already widespread in 2025.
insights.locator: Incidence of Tariff-Related Costs on Small Businesses

themes.theme: Firms split between passing on and absorbing costs

insights.claim: About 80% of goods and retail firms, both nationally and regionally, reported passing on at least some tariff-driven cost increases to customers, while about 60% reported absorbing at least some of the cost increases internally, with 36% of goods firms and 43% of retail firms nationally doing both.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Confirms tariff pass-through to consumers is the dominant small-business response but is only partial, meaning tariffs are simultaneously feeding inflation and compressing small-business margins.
insights.locator: Responding to Higher Imported Input Costs

insights.claim: Older and more profitable small firms were more inclined to pass tariff costs on to customers and less inclined to absorb them, likely because surviving past the early-bankruptcy period typical of young small firms signals greater pricing power.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Identifies which small businesses are most vulnerable to margin compression from tariffs — younger, less profitable firms bear a disproportionate share of the absorbed cost burden.
insights.locator: Responding to Higher Imported Input Costs

insights.claim: Large firms can mitigate the incidence of higher input prices from tariffs through legal means and generally have a greater ability to maintain price markups, whereas smaller, less profitable firms with fewer resources are less able to do so.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Tariffs likely widen the competitive gap between large and small firms within the same sector, a structural effect beyond the aggregate inflation impact.
insights.locator: Responding to Higher Imported Input Costs

themes.theme: Tariff pain is dragging down 2026 expectations

insights.claim: National firms that reported tariff-related challenges in 2025 were less likely to expect increased revenues or employment in 2026, even after controlling for firm age, revenues, profitability, and location, while broad inflation concerns were unrelated to employment and revenue expectations.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Tariff exposure specifically, not general inflation, is a leading indicator of weaker small-business hiring and revenue plans for 2026 — a concrete data point for forecasting small-business-driven employment growth.
insights.locator: How Did Tariff Challenges Affect Expectations of Firms’ Prospects in 2026?

insights.claim: Small businesses reported that, by a large margin, they expect their export sales to decrease rather than increase in 2026.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Signals a probable further contraction in small-business export activity in 2026, likely tied to retaliatory tariffs and trade friction.
insights.locator: How Did Tariff Challenges Affect Expectations of Firms’ Prospects in 2026?

insights.claim: Prior research found that larger declines in stock prices on tariff-announcement days in 2018-19 were associated with lower profits, employment, and labor productivity at affected firms in the following two years, suggesting today's tariff-related pessimism could translate into similarly durable real economic effects.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Provides a historical analog (2018-19 trade war) for how far current tariff-driven small-business pessimism could extend into actual employment and productivity declines.
insights.locator: How Did Tariff Challenges Affect Expectations of Firms’ Prospects in 2026?

insights.claim: Separate research cited in the post found that mid-sized firms (50-499 employees) paid sharply higher tariffs in 2025, consistent with this survey's findings on small-business tariff exposure.
insights.speaker: Elena Botogoeva
insights.so_what: Cross-validates the survey findings with independent tariff-payment data, strengthening confidence that mid-sized/smaller firms are bearing a disproportionate tariff burden.
insights.locator: Incidence of Tariff-Related Costs on Small Businesses

top_entities: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
